paper_id: Lu and Liu - 2025 - Communicating Concerns, Emotional Expressions, and Disparities on Ethnic Communities on Social Media
tei_path: /content/drive/MyDrive/content_analysis_epistemology/papers/tei/raw/social_sciences_interdisciplinary/Lu and Liu - 2025 - Communicating Concerns, Emotional Expressions, and Disparities on Ethnic Communities on Social Media.tei.xml

metadata.title: Communicating Concerns, Emotional Expressions, and Disparities on Ethnic Communities on Social Media During the COVID-19 Pandemic: A Structural Topic Modeling Approach

abstract: Ethnic and racial disparities in the coronavirus (COVID-19) pandemic raise significant concerns. This study analyzes social media discourses toward four ethnic communities in the United States during the pandemic and reveals disparities in pandemic experiences among them. A total of 488,029 tweets mentioning one of the four ethnic communities, that is, Asians, Blacks, Hispanics, and Native Americans, were investigated by a structural topic modeling approach with emotional expressions and time as covariates in the topic model. The results demonstrate that discourses about Asian, Hispanics, and Native American communities were often induced by pandemic-related events, concerning topics beyond one's community, and reflecting an experience of implicit racism and an adoption of technical supports from health systems. Meanwhile, discourses about Blacks were racially related, discussing topics within the community, and reflecting an experience of explicit racism and an adoption of psychological supports from ingroup. We discuss the implications of our findings on ethnic health disparities.

sections.paper_id: Lu and Liu - 2025 - Communicating Concerns, Emotional Expressions, and Disparities on Ethnic Communities on Social Media
paragraphs: The COVID-19 pandemic has not only posed unprecedented threats to global health, but also raised significant concerns about health disparities among different socioeconomic populations (e.g., Hawkins et al., 2020, p. 233), geographic groups (e.g., Hirko et al., 2020;Zhang et al., 2021), and racial and ethnic communities (e.g., Chowkwanyun & Reed, 2020). Yet, it remains relatively understudied regarding the differences about people's concerns, discourse, and reactions to COVID-19 toward distinct racial and ethnic communities, an issue that helps to identify and overcome disparities across ethnicities (for a general discussion on the COVID-related discourse, see, Jang et al., 2021). To fill the gap, we analyze COVID-19-related tweets in the United States between January 28, 2020 and December 31, 2020 with a structural topic modeling (STM) approach to map out different discourses on various ethnic communities and corresponding emotional expressions. Empirical findings have revealed COVID-19related topics and themes toward four communities of Asian, Black, Hispanic, and Native American (arranged alphabetically) as well as divergent emotional expressions, which sheds significant light on health related but also other disparities across these communities with plausible reasons.

Next, we first present a review of scholarship on public health and racial and ethnic disparities, also with a focus on the COVID-19 pandemic. We pinpoint two gaps: What are people's discourses on (1) these four ethnic communities, instead of the widely studied, yet narrowly focused black and white Americans, regarding (2) pandemic-related topics, along with their corresponding emotional expressions. Second, we explain our methodology. Third, we present our findings and discussion with explanations and reflections on our conclusions.

sections.paper_id: Lu and Liu - 2025 - Communicating Concerns, Emotional Expressions, and Disparities on Ethnic Communities on Social Media
sections.header_raw: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in the Public Health
sections.header_norm: racial and ethnic disparities in the public health
paragraphs: Besides gender and socioeconomic disparities, racial-ethnic disparities have a long history of being as a key topic in conversations about public health (for reviews, see Dressler et al., 2005;Williams & Collins, 1995). Disparities in this study speak to differences in healthcare understanding and utilization as well as subsequent health outcomes among distinct social groups (Kim et al., 2020(Kim et al., , p. 2441)). While early scholarship considered disparities as reflecting biological differences between racial groups (e.g., Krieger, 1987), later studies have acknowledged that disparities are largely socially, psychologically, and culturally constructed (Krieger, 2003). The structural-constructivist model addresses "the dual nature of human existence" (Dressler et al., 2005, p. 241) that influences health disparities. Dressler et al. (2005, p. 243) underlined the relevance of social determinants of racial and ethnic disparities in the public health, which entail "how life goals are culturally constructed within communities of color and the implications for health when those goals are limited by racial stratification."

The persistence of racial and ethnic disparities in the public health as a social construct has been exemplified in scholarships on the COVID-19 pandemic (e.g., Thakur et al., 2020). Some portray inequalities in COVID-19 infection and mortality rates, with particularly Blacks and Hispanic populations being with higher rates in comparison to other ethnic groups (e.g., Holtgrave et al., 2020). Others examine reasonings that underpin such disparities, such as cultural and structural racism (Razai et al., 2021) and social network resources (Gauthier et al., 2021).

Despite substantial contributions, existing scholarship on racial and ethnic disparities in the pandemic suffers from two limitations. For one thing, the discussion on health disparities between black and white Americans dominates extant scholarships, despite the fact that other racial and ethnic communities such as Hispanic and Asian Americans are also suffering from various degrees of vulnerability (Laurencin & McClinton, 2020;Wang et al., 2020). The review by Mackey et al. (2021) further confirmed that in the case of COVID-19, Asian, Pacific Islander, and other populations were "much less commonly reported" in comparison to African American/Black and Hispanic populations. Dressler et al. (2005) underlined that "understanding ethnic and racial health disparities demands a careful examination of all groups in all societies in which such disparities exist" (p. 233). A comprehensive view of health disparities during the COVID-19 pandemic thereby encompasses racial and ethnic communities beyond black and white Americans.

For another, on a general note, Niederdeppe et al. (2013, p. 10) pinpoint "surprisingly little research on public perceptions of health disparities." Such perception and awareness of health disparities are often manifested in public concerns, attitudes, and discourses in health situations. An investigation into those discourses helps evaluate the effects of communication about health disparities, for instance, in the mass media (e.g., Cho & Salmon, 2007;Niederdeppe et al., 2013), and would guide policymaking and communication campaign that have the potential to address and reduce health disparities. In the case of COVID-19, Campos-Castillo and Laestadius (2020) demonstrate that racial and ethnic minority groups are more likely to contribute to pandemicrelated content on social media than whites, which implies health disparities as dissimilar awareness and subsequently differing discourses of the pandemic between these minority groups and whites. Yet, few studies capture social media discourses from the public perspective that exemplifies disparities between racial groups in "pandemic experiences and how they are related to structural inequalities within the United States" (Campos-Castillo & Laestadius, 2020, p. 11).

Furthermore, we propose that emotional expressions in racial and ethnic-related discourses offer advanced insights into ethnic-related health disparity. Emotional expressions in discourses toward racially minority groups manifest how the society and/or the groups themselves perceive, frame, and respond to the emotional sufferings of the ethnic groups during the COVID-19 pandemic. On the one hand, positive expressions can reveal social resilience resources that the racial groups receive from the society or emerge within the groups (Garcia & Rimé, 2019;Iliev et al., 2016). Thus, disparities in positive expressions would likely reflect disparities in whether and how racial groups articulate social resilience resources. On the other hand, psychological research reveals that emotions indicate action tendencies, such that positive emotions (e.g., sympathy) imply approach behaviors (e.g., giving help) while negative emotions (e.g., anger and fear) promote offensive and avoidance behaviors (Fontaine & Scherer, 2013;Mackie et al., 2000;Weiner, 1980). The close relationships between emotions and actions indicate that racial groups would be treated correspondent to the emotional expressions in the ethnic-related discourses framed by the society. Therefore, an understanding of emotional expressions in discourses toward ethnic communities provides a more holistic view on disparities in pandemic experiences of ethnic and racial minorities.

To fill the abovementioned gaps, we explore the pandemic-related discussion and discourse toward four ethnic communities in the United States, as previous scholarship has (Laurencin & McClinton, 2020), with their corresponding emotional expressions on Twitter for characterizing disparities across ethnicities. As Niederdeppe et al. (2013, p. 9) suggest, social media offers new possibilities to explore public awareness and engagement in public health event. Such an interrogation allows us to identify, call attention to, and make salient, differences in public perception, concerns, and attitude toward different racial and ethnic communities during the pandemic. We ask the research question (RQ) and two sub-RQs: RQ: What are the different discourses and attitudes toward the Asian, Black, Hispanic, and Native American communities on Twitter that exemplify disparities across these communities? Sub-RQ1: Which topics and themes are salient about different ethnic communities during the pandemic? Sub-RQ2: How do different topics and themes about ethnic communities evolve across time with various emotional expressions?

sections.paper_id: Lu and Liu - 2025 - Communicating Concerns, Emotional Expressions, and Disparities on Ethnic Communities on Social Media
Lu and Liu - 2025 - Communicating Concerns, Emotional Expressions, and Disparities on Ethnic Communities on Social Media
paragraphs: We utilized a publicly available Twitter database for COVID-19 research created by Gupta et al., (2020). The database collected COVID-19-related English tweets across the globe from the Twitter Application Programming Interface. The most updated version of the database included tweets published from January 28 to December 31, 2020.

The dataset included labels of several attributes, including geolocation at the national level, sentiment valance, and emotional categories. Gupta and Yang (2018) utilize CrystalFree to evaluate the intensity of four basic emotions (anger, sadness, fear, and joy) in tweets and assigned them a corresponding dominant emotion category or otherwise in a "no specific emotion" category. In this study, we exploited the Twitter database published from the US, totally 38,031,772 tweets (Figure 1 for the study flow). We extracted racial-related tweets from this database regarding four ethnic communities, that is, Asian, Black, Hispanic, and Native American, using 234 search keywords in Nguyen et al. (2018Nguyen et al. ( , 2019)). The keyword list is in Appendix A.
sections.text:
We utilized a publicly available Twitter database for COVID-19 research created by Gupta et al., (2020). The database collected COVID-19-related English tweets across the globe from the Twitter Application Programming Interface. The most updated version of the database included tweets published from January 28 to December 31, 2020.

The dataset included labels of several attributes, including geolocation at the national level, sentiment valance, and emotional categories. Gupta and Yang (2018) utilize CrystalFree to evaluate the intensity of four basic emotions (anger, sadness, fear, and joy) in tweets and assigned them a corresponding dominant emotion category or otherwise in a "no specific emotion" category. In this study, we exploited the Twitter database published from the US, totally 38,031,772 tweets (Figure 1 for the study flow). We extracted racial-related tweets from this database regarding four ethnic communities, that is, Asian, Black, Hispanic, and Native American, using 234 search keywords in Nguyen et al. (2018Nguyen et al. ( , 2019)). The keyword list is in Appendix A.

sections.paper_id: Lu and Liu - 2025 - Communicating Concerns, Emotional Expressions, and Disparities on Ethnic Communities on Social Media
paragraphs: We applied the STM method with the "stm" package (Roberts et al., 2019) in R software. STM incorporates document attributes into the topic model and reveals how such attributes are associated with topical discourses and prevalence. To explore Twitter users' concerns, discourse, and attitudes toward different ethnical communities, we integrated two covariates, that is, emotional categories and timeline, into the STM. We further compared timelines of topics discussed on Twitter with the timing of key events to individual racial community. Tweets were analyzed in terms of individual ethnic groups. Before STM, we preprocessed the raw text data using the testProcessor algorithm within the stm package, which stems words, removes punctuations, and eliminates stop words. Tweets that did not contain any words after the preprocessing were automatically excluded from further modeling analyses (see Figure 1). After that, we estimated the structural topic model with emotional category and published time to predict the topical prevalence. The estimation required a predefined number (K) of topics. As such, we applied the searchK function to determine the number of topics by estimating stm models ranging from 3 to 20 topics and comparing model indexes like held-out likelihood, semantic coherence, and residuals. We selected the K model where index improvements were small with larger K (Blei et al., 2003). Once the number of topics was determined, we estimated 20 stm models with K topics with the selectModel function and selected the model with highest semantic coherence and exclusivity as the final topic modeling results. Then, we used the estimateEffect algorithms to identify the most prevalent emotions in each topic. We named each topic by examining keywords and representative tweets and summarized them into themes inductively.
sections.text: We applied the STM method with the "stm" package (Roberts et al., 2019) in R software. STM incorporates document attributes into the topic model and reveals how such attributes are associated with topical discourses and prevalence. To explore Twitter users' concerns, discourse, and attitudes toward different ethnical communities, we integrated two covariates, that is, emotional categories and timeline, into the STM. We further compared timelines of topics discussed on Twitter with the timing of key events to individual racial community. Tweets were analyzed in terms of individual ethnic groups. Before STM, we preprocessed the raw text data using the testProcessor algorithm within the stm package, which stems words, removes punctuations, and eliminates stop words. Tweets that did not contain any words after the preprocessing were automatically excluded from further modeling analyses (see Figure 1). After that, we estimated the structural topic model with emotional category and published time to predict the topical prevalence. The estimation required a predefined number (K) of topics. As such, we applied the searchK function to determine the number of topics by estimating stm models ranging from 3 to 20 topics and comparing model indexes like held-out likelihood, semantic coherence, and residuals. We selected the K model where index improvements were small with larger K (Blei et al., 2003). Once the number of topics was determined, we estimated 20 stm models with K topics with the selectModel function and selected the model with highest semantic coherence and exclusivity as the final topic modeling results. Then, we used the estimateEffect algorithms to identify the most prevalent emotions in each topic. We named each topic by examining keywords and representative tweets and summarized them into themes inductively.

Tweets that did not contain any words after the preprocessing were automatically excluded from further modeling analyses (see Figure 1). After that, we estimated the structural topic model with emotional category and published time to predict the topical prevalence. The estimation required a predefined number (K) of topics. As such, we applied the searchK function to determine the number of topics by estimating stm models ranging from 3 to 20 topics and comparing model indexes like held-out likelihood, semantic coherence, and residuals. We selected the K model where index improvements were small with larger K (Blei et al., 2003). Once the number of topics was determined, we estimated 20 stm models with K topics with the selectModel function and selected the model with highest semantic coherence and exclusivity as the final topic modeling results. Then, we used the estimateEffect algorithms to identify the most prevalent emotions in each topic. We named each topic by examining keywords and representative tweets and summarized them into themes inductively.
sections.preview_head: We applied the STM method with the "stm" package (Roberts et al., 2019) in R software. STM incorporates document attributes into the topic model and reveals how such attributes are associated with topical discourses and prevalence. To explore Twitter users' concerns, discourse, and attitudes toward different ethnical communities, we integrated two covariates, that is, emotional categories and timeline, into the STM.
sections.preview_tail: Once the number of topics was determined, we estimated 20 stm models with K topics with the selectModel function and selected the model with highest semantic coherence and exclusivity as the final topic modeling results. Then, we used the estimateEffect algorithms to identify the most prevalent emotions in each topic. We named each topic by examining keywords and representative tweets and summarized them into themes inductively.

tags.label: METHODS
tags.reason: Describes the application of Structural Topic Modeling (STM), including software, model selection, K

sections.paper_id: Lu and Liu - 2025 - Communicating Concerns, Emotional Expressions, and Disparities on Ethnic Communities on Social Media
paragraphs: The data extraction produced a total of 488,029 tweets containing at least one of the keywords pertaining to ethnic groups, including 270,923 tweets regarding the Asian community, 111,984 regarding Blacks, 76,411 regarding Hispanics, and 28,711 regarding Native Americans. After excluding tweets that contain no word after text preprocessing procedures, the stm algorithm included totally 270,910 tweets mentioning Asians, 111,983 tweets mentioning Blacks, 76,439 mentioning Hispanics, and 28,711 mentioning native Americans.

sections.paper_id: Lu and Liu - 2025 - Communicating Concerns, Emotional Expressions, and Disparities on Ethnic Communities on Social Media
Lu and Liu - 2025 - Communicating Concerns, Emotional Expressions, and Disparities on Ethnic Communities on Social Media
paragraphs: We identified three themes regarding Twitter discussions on different communities on the basis of stm algorithm-generated topics. The themes consist of pandemic-related, health-related, and racial-related topics (Figures 2a-5a). The pandemic-related theme concerns tweets on issues and events derived from the course of the pandemic. The health-related theme covers health issues concerning the community. The racialrelated theme speaks to race and ethnicity. Furthermore, in Figures 2 to 5, the varieties of the color blue represent diverse topics under the pandemic-related theme, including conspiracy theories on COVID origin (on the Asian community), coverage of the COVID-19 pandemic, and policy conflict (on the Native American community). Similarly, denoting the health-related theme, the varieties of the color yellow contain topics including health disparity, community health funding and support (on the Native American community), and vaccines (on the Asian community). The varieties of the color red involve topics in the racial-related theme, including (anti)-racism and anti-Asian hate (on the Asian community only).

The Pandemic-Related Theme. The tweets on the Asian, Hispanic, and Native American communities all covered the pandemic related theme, except those on the Black communities (see Figures 2a-5a). Within the pandemic-related theme, both common and specific topics regarding these three ethnic groups emerged. First, "coverage of the COVID-19 pandemic" was a topic spanning across the Asian, Hispanic, and Native American communities, with fear being prominent in this topic. Despite such commonality, the content of the tweets differed between different communities. Regarding the Hispanic and the Native American communities, this topical discourse reported predominantly COVID-19 cases within the community. In contrast, the discourse concerning the Asian community was mainly about the outbreak that originated in Wuhan, China, rather than the epidemic within the Asian (American) community. Second, the topic "COVID-19 hits" states how COVID-19 changed daily routines or personal lives, with sadness and anger being the two featured emotions. The topic emerged as a prominent one only among tweets on the Asian and the Native American communities, while the content also encapsulates differences between the two communities. For Native Americans, the discourse involved personal expression about the pandemic-induced disruption. For the Asian community, the discourse embraces responsibility evaluation processes exemplified by words such as blame and problem.

Third, several topics were featured in specific ethnical communities. The topic on "policy conflict" between the state government and the tribe governors turned out as a distinct topic in tweets about the Native American community. "Coverage of the COVID-19 pandemic -Brazilian president" was a topic about reports on the meeting between then-President Trump and the Brazilian President Bolsonaro. "Conspiracy theories on COVID origin," "COVID-19 restrictions," and "anti-Chinese government discourse" were three discrete topics in tweets referring to the Asian communities. These topics were not essentially related to the Asian-American community, but China.

The Health-Related Theme. "Community health" and "health disparity" come to light as two common topics across all four ethnic communities under the health-related theme (Figures 2a -5a). "Community health" embraces discourses about health relief programs, funds, and technical support provided to the ethnic communities. Joy was the only featured emotion in this topical discourse. In comparison, "health disparity" incorporates discourses about the comparison in prevalence and death rate of COVID-19 across ethnic groups, with fear as the commonly dominant emotion in this discourse.

Though these two topics shared across tweets regarding different ethnic communities, the discourses differed across communities in five ways. First, the topics about the Black community combine community health and disparity as one single topic, while those regarding the rest communities all have the two topics separately. This implies that the tweets on community health and disparity always came together in the discussion on the Black community. Second, "community health" for Native Americans emerged as two distinct topics, one about funds and donation and the other about technical support. Tweets regarding the other communities did not call for such specific issues. Third, the tweets on "health disparity" regarding the Black community concentrated on African Americans without mentioning the other ethnicities, while those on the rest communities involve comparisons of COVID-19 mortality rate across all ethnic communities. Fourth, under the topic "health disparity," discourses toward Native Americans further involve hashtags like "#NativeAmericanLivesMatter" and "#WhiteLivesMatter" together with "#BlackLivesMatter" and "#HispanicLivesMatter," while other discourses on the rest communities did not include such content. Fifth, tweets regarding the Asian community also hold a unique topic about vaccine collaboration and competition between the United States and China.

The Racial-Related Theme. Racial-related theme crystallizes as one significant issue in the COVID-19 discourses toward ethnic communities, yet with divergent contents. For the Asian community, the thematic discourses involved two distinct topics, one on racism and antiracism toward the Asian community and the other on the anti-Asian hate, which was related to then-President Trump's strident anti-China rhetoric. Both topics evoked anger as the dominant emotion. The discourses about racism toward the Black community are characterized by slang and swear words, also with anger being the prominent emotion. Furthermore, #BlackLivesMatter was a widespread hashtag within the anti-racist discourse. Moreover, cultural pride also emerged as a topic for the Black community. For the Hispanic community, the racism speeches occurred in the discussion about the coincidence of a Mexican beer under the name of corona. For the Native American community, racism discourses did not accumulate as a topic explicitly, yet are still reflected through the use of hashtags #NativeAmericanMatter and #AllLivesMatter.

tags.label: RESULTS
tags.reason: Reports findings from STM: identified themes, topics, and emotional expressions for each community

sections.paper_id: Lu and Liu - 2025 - Communicating Concerns, Emotional Expressions, and Disparities on Ethnic Communities on Social Media
sections.header_raw: The Temporal Trend of Themes and Topics
sections.header_norm: the temporal trend of themes and topics
paragraphs: Figures 2c to 5c visualize the temporal trends of different themes and topics on each individual ethnic community, corresponding with key events in the pandemic.

A detailed look at the figures exhibits that different topics on racial communities evolved along with key events in the pandemic. More specifically, the discourses toward the Asian community first peaked at the week when the United States declared the national emergency, skyrocketed after then President Trump's use of the terms "Chinese virus," and then increased again when Li-meng Yan asserted that the virus is a product of government-controlled laboratory modification. Tweets on the Black community increased four times, two of which were directly driven by racial-related topics including the COVID-19 pandemic's disproportionate damage in African American communities and the Black Lives Matter movement. The rest two were facilitated when World Health Organization (WHO) declared COVID-19 as a pandemic and when President Trump and First Lady Melania Trump both tested positive for COVID-19. The World Health Organization (WHO) declaration also triggered the first peak on Twitter on the Hispanic community. Then, the other two tweet peaks regarding the Hispanic community were racial specific and oriented. On the contrary, the tweet peak regarding Native Americans concerned mostly health-related, instead of racial and ethnicity-related topics.

sections.paper_id: Lu and Liu - 2025 - Communicating Concerns, Emotional Expressions, and Disparities on Ethnic Communities on Social Media
paragraphs: Racial and ethnic disparities have been one of the prominent issues in public health, with the ongoing COVID-19 pandemic drawing extensive and extra attention to such an issue (e.g., Holtgrave et al., 2020;Hooper et al., 2020). However, less is known about public perceptions of disparities across ethnic communities. With the tweets on different ethnic communities in the United States, we adopt the STM approach, combined with an interrogation of emotional expressions and a timeline of events, to delineate people's perceptions, concerns, and reactions during the pandemic on social media. Our findings reveal and enrich current discussions on disparities during a public health crisis in the following four aspects.

First, our findings accentuate health related, but also other disparities across ethnic communities and their plausible reasons. The concerns and reactions toward a disproportionate burden of COVID-19 morbidity and mortality in the Black and Hispanic communities exemplify societal, or structural racial inequality that generates "cumulative and chronic adverse outcomes" (Hardeman, 2020, p. 777) regarding health situations, among others, for people from these two communities. Instead of deeply seated structural racial inequality, the discourses on the Asian community are manipulated by specific political events, like Trump's "Chinese virus" slur, but also conspiracy theories of China spreading the virus intentionally, which is newly found in our study. Meanwhile, the conflict of central-local government policies fosters dominant discourses on the Native American community.

Second, our findings reveal disparities regarding if the discourses on one community goes beyond the specific community in question to other ethnic communities. More specifically, the discourses on the Black community revolve around topics within the community, like BlackLivesMatter and cultural pride advocacy, but they seldom cover issues in other ethnic communities. On the contrary, the discourses on the rest three communities, particularly those about health-related themes, contained not only within-community topics, but also beyond-community topics related to other communities. In other words, the discourse on the black community tends to be inward oriented, which concentrates on, and to a degree, is constrained by the community in question. On the contrary, the discourses on the other communities are outward oriented, which spans across and beyond the concern the community has. Such a difference could shed light on possible coalitions among these communities against (health) disparities, like the Advocacy and Education Outreach programs for both Asian and Pacific Islander (see Figure 5a, the topic of "Community health support").

Third, our findings highlight disparities in the experiences of racism and discrimination across different ethnic communities. The topic regarding racist speeches toward Blacks confirms explicit racism toward the Black community in American society during the pandemic. Studies have consistently showed that experiences of such explicit racism through verbal harassment are significant psychosocial stressors with aversive effects on health status, which further induces health-related risky behaviors (see Williams & Mohammed, 2013). This offers a potentially additional explanation other than structural inequity on why there was poor compliance with COVID-19 public health recommendations among African Americans (Block et al., 2020). In contrast, the Asian and Hispanic communities experienced racism and discriminations mainly in implicit forms, which often linked coronavirus to the communities such as the fear of getting infections after eating Chinese food or drinking Mexican corona beer. The Native Americans experienced an even more implicit form of discrimination manifested in the policy conflicts between the state government and local tribes. Though implicit racism may not be directly related to individual health outcomes, it could interfere with clinical assessment and provider-patient relationships (e.g., Cooper et al., 2012). In short, such differences in experiences of racism will have indications on the disparities in a very broad range of health outcomes among racial groups.

Fourth, distinctive emotional expressions articulated around diverse topics across ethnic communities further illustrate existing disparities among these communities. On the one hand, the findings showed that positive sentiments emerged for all racial groups, but in different forms. External supports from health systems seemed to be fundamental for Asian, Hispanic, and Native American groups. In comparison, internal and psychological supports, such as cultural pride and collaborative fights against racism (e.g., BlackLivesMatter movement), were salient for the Black community. These findings echo our other findings about disparities in experiences of inequality and adoptions of coping strategies for health issues between racial groups.

On the other hand, negative emotions dominated the ethnic-related discourses. The findings showed that anger emerged as one of the major emotional expressions toward Asian and Native American groups in both pandemic-related and healthrelated themes, while it did not surface regarding the African and Hispanic groups. These imply that contextually induced conflicts commonly existed for Asian and Native American groups during the pandemic. Such conflicts would potentially increase hostility toward these two groups (Nir et al., 2022) and reduce the financial and health aids they could receive from society during the pandemic (Adler et al., 2022;Politi et al., 2021). Our findings thereby advocate for monitoring public opinion and sentiment online to better cope with the disparities in reality during the pandemic.

To conclude, our study showed essential ethnic and racial disparities in health and other aspects that have health implications. While the Black community suffered fundamental structural inequality, racism, and discriminations and adopted an inward-oriented coping strategy, other ethnic communities experienced a more political-driven and implicit form of inequality and practiced an outward-oriented strategy with possible coalitions with other communities. Our study thus helps design strategic communication campaigns for reducing health disparities for different ethnic groups.

Our study represents an initial step in exploring social media discourse on disparities across ethnic communities with several limitations. First, Twitter is different from Facebook-like platforms where people are focusing on relationship maintenance and emotional exchanges. Thus, our Twitter dataset may be more able to reveal the structural inequality of pandemic experiences for racial communities, and less to demonstrate interpersonal factors that contributes to racial health disparities. Second, as users of Twitter skew toward a younger population, the discourses found in this study may not be representative for the United States. Third, social media discourse to capture concerns and attitudes related to ethnic groups is not able to measure subjective experiences of disparities for ethnic members.

In other words, the discourse on the black community tends to be inward oriented, which concentrates on, and to a degree, is constrained by the community in question. On the contrary, the discourses on the other communities are outward oriented, which spans across and beyond the concern the community has. Such a difference could shed light on possible coalitions among these communities against (health) disparities, like the Advocacy and Education Outreach programs for both Asian and Pacific Islander (see Figure 5a, the topic of "Community health support").

Third, our findings highlight disparities in the experiences of racism and discrimination across different ethnic communities. The topic regarding racist speeches toward Blacks confirms explicit racism toward the Black community in American society during the pandemic. Studies have consistently showed that experiences of such explicit racism through verbal harassment are significant psychosocial stressors with aversive effects on health status, which further induces health-related risky behaviors (see Williams & Mohammed, 2013). This offers a potentially additional explanation other than structural inequity on why there was poor compliance with COVID-19 public health recommendations among African Americans (Block et al., 2020). In contrast, the Asian and Hispanic communities experienced racism and discriminations mainly in implicit forms, which often linked coronavirus to the communities such as the fear of getting infections after eating Chinese food or drinking Mexican corona beer. The Native Americans experienced an even more implicit form of discrimination manifested in the policy conflicts between the state government and local tribes. Though implicit racism may not be directly related to individual health outcomes, it could interfere with clinical assessment and provider-patient relationships (e.g., Cooper et al., 2012). In short, such differences in experiences of racism will have indications on the disparities in a very broad range of health outcomes among racial groups.

Fourth, distinctive emotional expressions articulated around diverse topics across ethnic communities further illustrate existing disparities among these communities. On the one hand, the findings showed that positive sentiments emerged for all racial groups, but in different forms. External supports from health systems seemed to be fundamental for Asian, Hispanic, and Native American groups. In comparison, internal and psychological supports, such as cultural pride and collaborative fights against racism (e.g., BlackLivesMatter movement), were salient for the Black community. These findings echo our other findings about disparities in experiences of inequality and adoptions of coping strategies for health issues between racial groups.

On the other hand, negative emotions dominated the ethnic-related discourses. The findings showed that anger emerged as one of the major emotional expressions toward Asian and Native American groups in both pandemic-related and healthrelated themes, while it did not surface regarding the African and Hispanic groups. These imply that contextually induced conflicts commonly existed for Asian and Native American groups during the pandemic. Such conflicts would potentially increase hostility toward these two groups (Nir et al., 2022) and reduce the financial and health aids they could receive from society during the pandemic (Adler et al., 2022;Politi et al., 2021). Our findings thereby advocate for monitoring public opinion and sentiment online to better cope with the disparities in reality during the pandemic.

To conclude, our study showed essential ethnic and racial disparities in health and other aspects that have health implications. While the Black community suffered fundamental structural inequality, racism, and discriminations and adopted an inward-oriented coping strategy, other ethnic communities experienced a more political-driven and implicit form of inequality and practiced an outward-oriented strategy with possible coalitions with other communities. Our study thus helps design strategic communication campaigns for reducing health disparities for different ethnic groups.

Our study represents an initial step in exploring social media discourse on disparities across ethnic communities with several limitations. First, Twitter is different from Facebook-like platforms where people are focusing on relationship maintenance and emotional exchanges. Thus, our Twitter dataset may be more able to reveal the structural inequality of pandemic experiences for racial communities, and less to demonstrate interpersonal factors that contributes to racial health disparities. Second, as users of Twitter skew toward a younger population, the discourses found in this study may not be representative for the United States. Third, social media discourse to capture concerns and attitudes related to ethnic groups is not able to measure subjective experiences of disparities for ethnic members.
sections.text_with_footnotes:
Racial and ethnic disparities have been one of the prominent issues in public health, with the ongoing COVID-19 pandemic drawing extensive and extra attention to such an issue (e.g., Holtgrave et al., 2020;Hooper et al., 2020). However, less is known about public perceptions of disparities across ethnic communities. With the tweets on different ethnic communities in the United States, we adopt the STM approach, combined with an interrogation of emotional expressions and a timeline of events, to delineate people's perceptions, concerns, and reactions during the pandemic on social media. Our findings reveal and enrich current discussions on disparities during a public health crisis in the following four aspects.

First, our findings accentuate health related, but also other disparities across ethnic communities and their plausible reasons. The concerns and reactions toward a disproportionate burden of COVID-19 morbidity and mortality in the Black and Hispanic communities exemplify societal, or structural racial inequality that generates "cumulative and chronic adverse outcomes" (Hardeman, 2020, p. 777) regarding health situations, among others, for people from these two communities. Instead of deeply seated structural racial inequality, the discourses on the Asian community are manipulated by specific political events, like Trump's "Chinese virus" slur, but also conspiracy theories of China spreading the virus intentionally, which is newly found in our study. Meanwhile, the conflict of central-local government policies fosters dominant discourses on the Native American community.

Second, our findings reveal disparities regarding if the discourses on one community goes beyond the specific community in question to other ethnic communities. More specifically, the discourses on the Black community revolve around topics within the community, like BlackLivesMatter and cultural pride advocacy, but they seldom cover issues in other ethnic communities. On the contrary, the discourses on the rest three communities, particularly those about health-related themes, contained not only within-community topics, but also beyond-community topics related to other communities. In other words, the discourse on the black community tends to be inward oriented, which concentrates on, and to a degree, is constrained by the community in question. On the contrary, the discourses on the other communities are outward oriented, which spans across and beyond the concern the community has. Such a difference could shed light on possible coalitions among these communities against (health) disparities, like the Advocacy and Education Outreach programs for both Asian and Pacific Islander (see Figure 5a, the topic of "Community health support").

Third, our findings highlight disparities in the experiences of racism and discrimination across different ethnic communities. The topic regarding racist speeches toward Blacks confirms explicit racism toward the Black community in American society during the pandemic. Studies have consistently showed that experiences of such explicit racism through verbal harassment are significant psychosocial stressors with aversive effects on health status, which further induces health-related risky behaviors (see Williams & Mohammed, 2013). This offers a potentially additional explanation other than structural inequity on why there was poor compliance with COVID-19 public health recommendations among African Americans (Block et al., 2020). In contrast, the Asian and Hispanic communities experienced racism and discriminations mainly in implicit forms, which often linked coronavirus to the communities such as the fear of getting infections after eating Chinese food or drinking Mexican corona beer. The Native Americans experienced an even more implicit form of discrimination manifested in the policy conflicts between the state government and local tribes. Though implicit racism may not be directly related to individual health outcomes, it could interfere with clinical assessment and provider-patient relationships (e.g., Cooper et al., 2012). In short, such differences in experiences of racism will have indications on the disparities in a very broad range of health outcomes among racial groups.

Fourth, distinctive emotional expressions articulated around diverse topics across ethnic communities further illustrate existing disparities among these communities. On the one hand, the findings showed that positive sentiments emerged for all racial groups, but in different forms. External supports from health systems seemed to be fundamental for Asian, Hispanic, and Native American groups. In comparison, internal and psychological supports, such as cultural pride and collaborative fights against racism (e.g., BlackLivesMatter movement), were salient for the Black community. These findings echo our other findings about disparities in experiences of inequality and adoptions of coping strategies for health issues between racial groups.

On the other hand, negative emotions dominated the ethnic-related discourses. The findings showed that anger emerged as one of the major emotional expressions toward Asian and Native American groups in both pandemic-related and healthrelated themes, while it did not surface regarding the African and Hispanic groups. These imply that contextually induced conflicts commonly existed for Asian and Native American groups during the pandemic. Such conflicts would potentially increase hostility toward these two groups (Nir et al., 2022) and reduce the financial and health aids they could receive from society during the pandemic (Adler et al., 2022;Politi et al., 2021). Our findings thereby advocate for monitoring public opinion and sentiment online to better cope with the disparities in reality during the pandemic.

To conclude, our study showed essential ethnic and racial disparities in health and other aspects that have health implications. While the Black community suffered fundamental structural inequality, racism, and discriminations and adopted an inward-oriented coping strategy, other ethnic communities experienced a more political-driven and implicit form of inequality and practiced an outward-oriented strategy with possible coalitions with other communities. Our study thus helps design strategic communication campaigns for reducing health disparities for different ethnic groups.

Our study represents an initial step in exploring social media discourse on disparities across ethnic communities with several limitations. First, Twitter is different from Facebook-like platforms where people are focusing on relationship maintenance and emotional exchanges. Thus, our Twitter dataset may be more able to reveal the structural inequality of pandemic experiences for racial communities, and less to demonstrate interpersonal factors that contributes to racial health disparities. Second, as users of Twitter skew toward a younger population, the discourses found in this study may not be representative for the United States. Third, social media discourse to capture concerns and attitudes related to ethnic groups is not able to measure subjective experiences of disparities for ethnic members.
